name: J.B. Rhine
description: Joseph Banks Rhine (1895-1980) was an American parapsychologist who pioneered the scientific study of extrasensory perception (ESP) and psychokinesis (PK). Rhine, along with his wife Louisa E. Rhine, established the parapsychology laboratory at Duke University in the 1930s. They conducted extensive experiments using card-guessing (Zener cards) and dice-rolling to test for ESP abilities like telepathy, clairvoyance, precognition, and retrocognition, and PK abilities. Rhine claimed to have found statistically significant evidence supporting the existence of these abilities, suggesting that the mind could influence the external world beyond known physical laws. His work sparked widespread public interest in parapsychology and led to the establishment of numerous parapsychology research centers. However, Rhine's methodologies and findings have been heavily criticized by mainstream scientists. Critics argue that his experiments were flawed due to methodological weaknesses, lack of proper controls, statistical misinterpretations, and potential for fraud or unintentional bias. Replications of Rhine's experiments by other researchers have generally failed to produce similar results. Despite the criticisms, Rhine's work remains a significant part of the history of parapsychology, inspiring further research and debate on the nature of consciousness and the potential for paranormal phenomena. Mainstream science generally considers ESP and PK to be pseudoscientific due to the lack of consistent and replicable evidence.
subtitle: Pioneer of Parapsychology Research

claims.claim: ESP exists and can be scientifically measured.
claims.evidence: Statistically significant results from Zener card experiments and other tests conducted at Duke University.
claims.criticism: Methodological flaws in experimental design, lack of proper controls, statistical misinterpretations, and failure to replicate results by independent researchers.

claims.evidence: Statistical anomalies observed in dice-rolling experiments, suggesting that subjects could influence the outcome of random events.
claims.criticism: Concerns about experimental bias, inadequate controls, and the possibility of unintentional manipulation of the dice. Replication failures are common.

claims.claim: The mind can influence the physical world beyond known physical laws.
claims.evidence: Correlation between mental states and observed outcomes in ESP and PK experiments.
claims.criticism: The proposed mechanism for how the mind could influence the physical world is undefined and violates established principles of physics. Occam's Razor suggests simpler explanations for the observed results.

keyFigures.name: J.B. Rhine
keyFigures.role: Pioneer of parapsychology research and founder of the Duke University parapsychology laboratory.

keyFigures.name: Louisa E. Rhine
keyFigures.role: Parapsychologist and collaborator with J.B. Rhine, focusing on qualitative aspects of ESP experiences.

keyFigures.name: C.E.M. Hansel
keyFigures.role: Psychologist and prominent critic of Rhine's research, pointing out methodological flaws and potential biases.

mythologyConnections: Ancient beliefs in divination and prophecy
Shamanistic practices involving altered states of consciousness

timeline.event: Rhine establishes the parapsychology laboratory at Duke University.
Rhine founds the Foundation for Research on the Nature of Man (FRNM).

sources.author: Deborah Blum
sources.work: Ghost Hunters: William James and the Search for Scientific Proof of Life After Death
sources.title: Deborah Blum
